speaker: interviewer
text: So you're out of the band. You're back to the regiment.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm, I'm, I'm now with the 50th RTR Regiment, and my number is Trooper Gilbert Zed. You see, in England, you don't say 'zee,' you say 'zed.'

speaker: interviewer
text: Zed.

speaker: interviewee
text: Trooper Gilbert Z, number 7918911, 50th Royal Tank Regiment, 7th Armored Brigade. And we were sent out as reinforcements to join the 8th Army. And at that time, the tanks and everything was coming in from America [HEBREW], everything. But we had to sail, and we didn't go by the Mediterranean. We went right round Africa, right up Aden, right up the Red Sea, to Port Said. And from there-- and I was stationed at the, at the regiment that we were acclimatized to being in, in Egypt, at [NON-ENGLISH]. That was the name of the camp, [NON-ENGLISH]. And then, I was at El Alamein all the way through, as I showed you on the map.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was in what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in, it was in 19-- 1972.

speaker: interviewee
text: Fool. And then the American Second Army came in from the other side under General Mark Clark with Patton and the others. And then it was a cake bomb. And then, the Germans, as what could, we took a lot of prisoners. And out of all the panzers that Germany had in the desert, there was only 15 left. All the others were shot to pieces. And then, we followed through to Sicily and then into Italy. And it was-- you know-- I was at Salerno and then later at Anzio. It was in July of 1944 that Rome had fallen, you know. But in the meantime-- one of the-- D-Day was June 6, 1944-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] just before then, one of the last V2s unfortunately killed my wife. Luckily, my daughter was in school. And I was informed. And I was given compassionate leave. And I went, I went with an American post plane. You know, when you send an airmail-- that started during the war, the airmail letters that you get, you know. That started during the war. They did it on film. And then, they-- you wrote a letter. It was filmed. And then, it was on a, on a, a cassette. And then, that was flown to England, printed, and then sent wherever it had to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I was on one of those planes. Came back to London and got my daughter. And she, together with my mother [INAUDIBLE] and father [INAUDIBLE], they looked after her. You see? Went back to Italy. Went back to Italy and, of course, you're in a transit camp. That's where I wasn't ready. Before you get to that, you mention the fact that your daughter was spared the V2 attack. How's--

speaker: interviewee
text: She was in school.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was your daughter born?

speaker: interviewee
text: My daughter was born September the 8th, 1936. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that's her daughter. That's my, that's my, that's my daughter up there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she was born in 1936.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, in Acre Lane Brixton Lying-in Home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm. So when this V2 unfortunately killed your wife, you--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was May 14, 1944. As you saw, I still have the-- I'm waiting to light the Yahrzeit light. Then I went back. And you know when you're separated from your regiment, it takes time for you to get there. By this time, when I got back, the Germans were on the run. Rome had been liberated in July. They were on the run.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was no [NON-ENGLISH]. And to find where your regiment is, do me a favor. By the time you get back to your regiment-- so one day, I went out for a walk after duty, which finish of duty was 4:00. I don't know which way I was going, but it was such a beautiful day. I went out for this walk.

speaker: interviewee
text: And all at once, I came across a camp, which had Jewish-- and people looking through the gates. And I went up to them and said, [HEBREW]. And immediately, there was a-- immediately there was a whole [HEBREW], a whole [HEBREW], said [SPEAKING HEBREW]. And then, somebody ran into the main office, and the, the chaplain came out, you know. And he spoke to me, you know. I told him-- you know, I gave him what I could. I had my cigarettes. I gave him my cigarettes and everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there were four other boys that were Jewish boys in this camp. And he said to me, we need help. This camp was a concentration camp, but it's now a transit camp. We're getting-- and he says, we're so overloaded, please, please, [HEBREW], come and help us out. Because they can say it in Yiddish. They can say it in French. Because they came from everywhere, you see? These were survivors from camps--

speaker: interviewee
text: These were the survivors from camps that were being moved down to send to Cypress. And I had to write out the things. I felt very embarrassed, because they used to kiss my hand every time I wrote, you know. And the four boys also came in to help. Then that was the only time. But it doesn't seem to-- people don't seem to realize there were concentration camps in Italy, too. Because on May the 8th--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, while I was in the camp, knowing I was a musician, they asked me to join a camp band, because I was having a ball. You know what I mean? So May the 8th was VE Day. So me-- Sergeant Gilbert-- the boys are down in the town in Rieti getting drunk. Nobody's going to crime them for it, but you're going to do guard. So got together four-- they got together four men and me to go on a 48-hour guard up to a place that was in the mountains-- because Rieti is in the high ground-- called Terminillo. And when I got up to Terminillo-- because it became a tank park after it was cleared out and washed down. In Terminillo was a tank park. And the reason we went up there, we were afraid of saboteurs, you know. And we were told to shoot first and ask questions later if we saw something around. But this tank park, this, this-- the buildings that these people were living in-- I never saw anything like it. You know when you go to a supermarket how-- this is the way I can only describe it. You know when you go to a supermarket and you see the produce and it's on a slant there and you pick what you want? The distance of this here, this table here was in between three layers in which the people were put to sleep after they came in tired, and their bodies keeping them warm, because you must remember it was up in the mountains. And they put straw out. They said, oh, you can sleep in the straw? I said, [NON-ENGLISH]. I am not going to sleep there. I've got my bedroll, you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, from there, I was-- got to my regiment--

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, this place, that you-- in Terminillo, that was also a--

speaker: interviewee
text: A concentration camp--

speaker: interviewer
text: A concentration camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: --that had been cleared already. You see? Nobody'd heard about it. And then, I was sent to Greece, to Salonika. From Salonika, I was sent up to-- up north. I'll remember the name in a minute. Gee, I'm getting too old. And we were sent up there, you know, to, like--

speaker: interviewer
text: In Greece?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Greece, to police the border-- Macedonia-- and all there. Florina was the name of the place, Florina. And it was from there that I finished my service, because the regiment was standing-- what they call 'standing down,' breaking up. Came back to England.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was in-- when?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- this was in Florina and Thessaloniki in Salonika.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this? In late 1945?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was '45. That was already December '45. You see, everybody-- every soldier-- had a number. It meant-- it was from one onwards. Because it meant years of service and age counted. My number was 24. When I was in Rieti, before I went to Greece-- before I went to Greece-- our orders-- you read orders every morning. If you don't, you're for it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the orders came up that all personnel from the numbers 1 to 26, inclusive, will be repatriated for demobilization, and those from 27 onwards will hold themselves in readiness to be sent to the Far East. Because the war wasn't over with Japan. And they will be demobilized at the rate they were mobilized, which meant one number every week. Which meant that I'd be home for Christmas, you see? So when the time came for me to go, it took 10 days-- from December the 10th till-- I was demobilized December the 20th. We went right down to Athens, from Athens to Pireaus, from Pireaus to Bari in Italy, by boat, from Italy right up to Milan, and from Milan, by train, through Switzerland, Lausanne, from France to Callais, and then from Callais to Guildford, where there was a whole show of civilian clothes that I wouldn't give to a dog. Because who had civilian clothes during the war, nice civilian clothes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyhow, I got demobilized. I came home, out of the Army. And I'd always kept in touch with the guys in the business. And no sooner did I get home, got my daughter, went to Mom and Dad, went to Mom and Dad with my daughter, I phoned the boys that I was home. They said, thank God you're home! Come in to work tomorrow. Because you see, I was very, very fortunate. Amongst the number-one musicians in London, they gave an audition to the guard's bands, which meant they had to sign up for 12 years or any part thereof before they were accepted. But they were stationed in Buckingham Palace, which was Birdcage Walk. They were stationed at the Tower of London. They were stationed at Windsor, so they could come in and do their jobs.

speaker: interviewer
text: But after the war finished, these guys couldn't do anything, because they had to do victory parades. So that's where I came in, started to earn money. You see? And then, those guys bought themselves out. So whatever they earned, they paid for it to get out the Army. But anyhow, I came home. I started work and saw that my daughter went to a boarding school in Byfleet, which is in Surrey. And that was costing 15 pounds a semester, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And everything went on fine. I was making money and doing very, very well. And then, well, I was working at all the number-one places in England, all the number-one. And you see, they have a thing in all the number-one hotels. From all over the world, they exchange waiters, musicians. Because it is known that if you work there, you're a person that they don't have to worry about. Anyhow, as I was working at the Cafe Anglais in London in Leicester Square, Geraldo-- who was one of the big booking bandleaders at that time-- got the contract with Cunard to supply music. The Queen Mary, at that time, was being refitted from being a trooper and was repainted and refitted and everything. And I got the job on the ship, first, as a musician in the band, then later as a bandleader on the Queen Mary. And we, we arrived in-- we-- they, they brought us in July, and we did a shakedown cruise in the, the North Sea. We then sailed from England. And I arrived in New York on August the 5th, 1947. Whilst the ship was going and coming in the Hudson, we were met by these fire floats and everybody, and sight-seeing and everybody. The newly painted Queen Mary was coming in, et cetera, et cetera. And I was dressed in a suit that was 22 ounces to the pound, you know, of material, you know, with a stiff collar-- very, very British. When the ship stopped, I thought I was going to die. It was like I was in an oven. Because it was 93 degrees and the-- what do you-- the humidity was 90.

speaker: interviewer
text: My cousin was waiting for me. When he saw me coming out, you know-- when I came out from the dock side at Pier 90, at Pier 90 at 50th Street, he said, my god, come on we're going to go-- you got money? Come. We'll go to Macy's. I'll buy you a tropical suit. So you spent most of your career afterwards leading bands--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --on these cruise ships?

speaker: interviewee
text: They weren't cruise ships.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were--

speaker: interviewee
text: They were--

speaker: interviewer
text: --passenger--

speaker: interviewee
text: They were passengers. The only ship that I was on was a passenger ship. But in the winter-- that was on the Mauritania-- that did cruises to the Caribbean, to Havana-- uh, what's the-- Caracas, Nassau, Curacao, and all these things.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did that-- wait a minute. Oh! I did another journey. After I did the Cunard, I did another journey to South America.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm. But--

speaker: interviewee
text: Coming back in 1949.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, how long did you spend on these passenger ships? Was that your whole career?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was my home. You only got time off when you docked. They turned you around quickly. You're gone.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about your daughter. Did--

speaker: interviewee
text: My daughter, at the time-- when I was on the Queen Mary-- my aunt from Great Barrington came down to see me. Because when I arrived in New York, she came from Great Barrington, Massachusetts. And I told her this story. You know, she knew the story already. And she says, bring her here. And she'll [HEBREW] with the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: I will bring her up.

speaker: interviewee
text: We will bring her up with a family, her cousins. Because my aunt and uncle-- and they incidentally died out here in California, in the valley-- they had a chicken farm, which they used to sell the eggs, in Great Barrington, Massachusetts. So I arranged when I got home, I said, Mama, I'm sending Jackie to America to the [INAUDIBLE]. She says, [HEBREW]. Because they were getting old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Surely.

speaker: interviewee
text: You see? And there comes a time-- she was 11 years old-- a girl's got to be ready for anything that happens physically.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what year was it when she came to--

speaker: interviewee
text: And this was-- so I arranged it with the, with the, with BOAC at that time that she leave London under the stewardess' supervision, and she would arrive the same day as the ship got into New York, with my fingers crossed that the ship was going to arrive on time. Because sometimes with the bad weather, even though we arrived late, we sailed on time. The ship arrived on time. God [HEBREW]. My aunt and uncle were waiting for me. And straight away, they drove us over to LaGuardia. At that time, LaGuardia was the--

speaker: interviewer
text: International airport.

speaker: interviewee
text: --international airport. I'm up on the balcony, watching the people coming in. And as my daughter comes in on the hand of the stewardess, I give her the whistle. She said, that's my daddy. That's my daddy. I ran down quickly. And I had to sign, you know. The stewardess gave me to her, introduced her to her uncle and aunt. We went out. We had something to eat. We went to see the [NON-ENGLISH], which was my mother's sister. And you know-- and then they went up to Massachusetts. And every time I used to get into port we used to-- if I got into port and we were in for more than a day and a half or two and a half, I used to take the train from Penn Central Station. I used to take the train to Great Barrington in Massachusetts to see her. And she did very well. She went to college in Boston. And there, she met a young man who was a pharmacist, a chemist. And they fell in love with each other. This was in 19-- but there was a lot happened during that time. Because after she had gone, then in 19-- I came back from South America in 1949. In 1949, I worked in England.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, how long did you-- on, on these passenger-- you spent your career working on these passenger ships--

speaker: interviewee
text: On these passenger ships till 1949 from 1947.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then, from 1949, what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: From 1949, I was working at the Savoy Hotel in London, the Barclay Hotel, and Claridges.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did that go on?

speaker: interviewee
text: That went on until I got the offer to go to Nairobi. In August-- I could show you it. I've got it in print. On August, I left England. I finished at the Barclay Hotel on Saturday the 27th of August and flew away from England to Nairobi on the 30th.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you in Nairobi?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two years and a quarter.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, I came back and worked again at the hotels, you know. And an offer came for me to take the band to the Taj Mahal Hotel in Bombay, which was celebrating its golden anniversary. And I went to the Taj Mahal Hotel in Bombay.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was there from September-- yeah, we arrived-- from September through to the end of February. And I came back to England the end of March of 1954.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then, where did--

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I worked once again in London. And then, the time came when I joined an act on the stage, and we toured Europe, went on a tour to Europe. But this is a thing I must tell you. In India, we were told not to eat out. It was dangerous. But I went into a cafe one night, and I saw something that I fancied very much. And what happened to me shouldn't happen to a dog. It developed into an anal fissure. And while I was on tour with the act that you see in the picture up there, we were befriended by a surgeon in Switzerland. And he said, in German, [GERMAN], it must be operated on. I came back to England. And of course, you know, being a musician is a young man's job. The only man that has the privilege of getting older is the bandleader. And the reason that I was being sent all over the world was, in England, I was told, Zan, you've got everything to be a bandleader. That's why we're sending you out. But you can't be a bandleader here, because we're closed shut. So when I was in hospital--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, while I was in Kenya, in Nairobi, I met-- I went to play at Dr. Williamson's diamond mines in Tanganyika. And there, I met a little Jewish man, a Dutch, Henry Abrams, who had been given the job by Harry Winston-- recommended-- valuing the stones as they came out of the ground. And we became very, very friendly. And he said, why don't you come to Detroit? You'll do great.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was in hospital, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it came to me. I was already married again, but I don't want to talk about that. It was a failure. And then, when I was in hospital, I said, enough. I'm going to go. This was in February, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 1958. I've got it in my diary in there if you want to see it. And at that time, when I went into hospital, I was playing with George Melachrino. I don't know if you ever heard that name. Very big band in England and nationwide. And I went into the hospital, which was the Middlesex Hospital in the Cavendish-Bentinck Ward, which was for these kind of things. And the doctor said to me, you are lucky.

speaker: interviewer
text: You are very lucky. If this has gone another six months, you would have had to have a colostomy. But as it is, they operated on me and said, you've got to be very, very clean at any time you have to go to the-- and if you go to my bathroom, you see I have a special rag, because I don't have a bidet. You know what I mean? And I came to America. You came to America when?

speaker: interviewee
text: As, as, as soon as I, as soon as I let Henry know-- Henry Abrams was this man's name. He was back in America. As soon as I let him know that I wanted to come to America, he stood up for me. And the papers came in for me, bang, bang, bang, even when I was in hospital. I came out of hospital. Then I applied for-- to come to America.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you arrive in America?

speaker: interviewee
text: I arrived in America September the 10th, 1958, the day before my birthday.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you went to Detroit?

speaker: interviewee
text: Went to Detroit immediately.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you started to work there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wait a minute. When I went to Detroit, I took a look at this city, me being a big city working-- I thought, where have I come to? Oye, no. I didn't like at all, because it was a big factory town. I have to get a job.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Henry says, I'm working at a discount store. I was working for $1.25 an hour. Because when you're not working, you're spending. And when you're working, you're earning, never mind what.

speaker: interviewee
text: I go to work in this place, Halls Discount Store, it was called. And then I went downtown looking for a job. And a guy told me that there was a man who sold Selmer instruments-- that he was looking for a salesman. The one he got, he didn't like. And I went in and applied.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the man said to me, what do you know about Selmer instruments? I said, first of all, I'm a professional musician. Next, I used to sell Selmer instruments in London, which I didn't. And the guy in London [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was a personal friend of mine. And when I told him who I'd worked for, he said, I know him. We have met on many a seminar, you see? When I got home, it came to my mind that this guy was going to write to Lou Davis. So I wrote an airmail letter to Lou Davis. And I said, I've applied for a job here, and I told him I'd worked for you. I'd be very happy if you'd give me a good [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] letter, you see. A letter came to me that morning-- an airmail letter-- that was a copy, a carbon copy-- at that time, they weren't using Xerox. It was a carbon copy of the letter he sent back that, if he engaged me, his gain would be his loss, you see? And as I'm working in the store, I get a telephone call. Listen to this story. I get a telephone call, can you come in to work tomorrow? And I said, yes. And I told Henry. He says, go. But he said to me-- I said to him, how much should I ask for wages? Because I wasn't acquainted with the wage scale. So he says, you ask for $100 a week, you see. So it came out-- with the take-out pay in taxes-- I was getting $82.03, which you still could make a living at that time. How long did you work at--

speaker: interviewee
text: Wait a minute. Wait a minute. I went into work there on a Thursday. On the Friday, the boss was in to show me the things-- a delta-winged bomber from the Royal Air Force in England was coming on a, on a-- what's the name-- a visit to America and fell on his house and, and destroyed it entirely. I put my jacket on. He said, no. You saved our lives. Don't do this. Because we're usually home doing our laundry on this Friday. But because you're here, it saved our lives. It was--

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll continue in a minute.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.
